title: Deep Vein Thrombosis
objectives: Apply Wells criteria for DVT probability assessment

vignette.text:
A 58-year-old woman presents with 3 days of left leg swelling and pain. She had knee replacement surgery 3 weeks ago. She has been less active since surgery.

Past Medical History: Osteoarthritis, Hypertension
Medications: Lisinopril 10mg daily, was on enoxaparin post-op but stopped 1 week ago
Social History: Non-smoker, no recent travel

physicalExam.extremities: Left leg: 3 cm larger circumference than right at calf, warmth, mild erythema, tenderness along deep veins, no cords palpable. Right leg normal.

questions.question: Calculate this patient's Wells score for DVT:
questions.explanation: Wells criteria: Recent surgery +1, Localized tenderness +1, Entire leg swollen +1, Calf >3cm difference +1, Pitting edema +1 = 5 points (high probability). Alternative diagnosis less likely adds +1 if applicable.

questions.question: With high Wells score, what is the next step?
questions.explanation: High probability Wells → go directly to compression ultrasound. D-dimer is only useful to rule out DVT in LOW probability patients. High probability needs imaging regardless of D-dimer.

questions.question: What is appropriate initial anticoagulation for acute proximal DVT?
questions.explanation: DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) are first-line for most patients with acute DVT. Can be started without bridging (unlike warfarin). Exceptions: cancer (LMWH preferred), renal failure, pregnancy.

questions.question: Standard duration of anticoagulation for provoked DVT (surgery)?
questions.explanation: Provoked DVT (major transient risk factor like surgery) = 3 months. Unprovoked DVT = consider extended therapy. Cancer-associated = treat while cancer active. Recurrent unprovoked = usually lifelong.

keyTakeaways: D-dimer useful only in LOW probability patients
DOACs first-line for most acute DVT
Duration: provoked = 3 months; unprovoked = consider extended
IVC filter only if anticoagulation contraindicated
